title: Make sitemap.ts and robots.ts request-host-aware
description:
## Context

`src/app/sitemap.ts:7` and `src/app/robots.ts:44` hardcode `https://www.exponential.im`. Preview deploys and staging environments emit a sitemap that points back to production — which is a real SEO and QA bug today (search engines and ops dashboards see wrong URLs). Making them host-aware fixes that and aligns them with the rest of the URL centralization work.

## Scope

1. Update `src/app/sitemap.ts` to build absolute URLs from `headers().get('host')` (via `next/headers`) — or from `getPublicBaseUrl()` if exponential-x4z0 has already introduced it. Prefer the helper if available.
2. Same for `src/app/robots.ts`: the `Sitemap:` directive should reference the current host.
3. Handle the protocol correctly: `x-forwarded-proto` header if present, otherwise default to `https` in prod, `http` in dev.

## Files to modify

- `src/app/sitemap.ts`
- `src/app/robots.ts`

## Dependencies / coordination

- Soft dependency on exponential-x4z0 (URL helper). If that bead is done first, use `getPublicBaseUrl()` directly. If not, duplicate the logic locally — exponential-x4z0's implementer will collapse them later.

## Verification

- Visit `http://localhost:3000/sitemap.xml` → all `<loc>` entries start with `http://localhost:3000`
- Visit `http://localhost:3000/robots.txt` → `Sitemap:` line points to `http://localhost:3000/sitemap.xml`
- `curl -H 'Host: staging.example.com' -H 'X-Forwarded-Proto: https' http://localhost:3000/robots.txt` → reflects `https://staging.example.com`
- Deploy preview on Vercel: sitemap should reflect the preview URL, not production
- `npm run check` passes

## Notes for implementer

- `headers()` in app-router handlers is async in Next 15 — make sure you `await` it
- Don't break the signature Next.js expects (`export default function sitemap(): MetadataRoute.Sitemap`) — the function can still be async
- Keep the list of sitemap entries the same; only change how absolute URLs are built
close_reason: Closed

title: Stop duplicating SECURITY_POLICY in both chat/stream route and every agent's instructions
description:
SECURITY_POLICY (~148 lines) is injected twice per Anthropic call:

1. Server-side in src/app/api/chat/stream/route.ts:290 — prepended as the highest-priority system message
2. Embedded inside each Mastra agent's static instructions:
   - mastra/src/mastra/agents/zoe-agent.ts (imports from ../agents/security-policy)
   - mastra/src/mastra/agents/assistant-agent.ts (imports from ../agents/security-policy)
   - mastra/src/mastra/agents/index.ts (Paddy/projectManager)

Both import from mastra/src/mastra/agents/security-policy.ts.

Pick ONE location. Recommended: keep it in the Mastra agent instructions only, and remove the server-side injection at stream/route.ts:290. Reasoning:
- Mastra agents are reused across multiple transports (chat/stream, Slack webhook, Telegram, WhatsApp, Signal). Only the stream route injects the policy server-side; the other transports already rely on the agent-embedded copy. Keeping the policy in the agent guarantees consistent security across all transports.
- The stream route's 'inject at top' pattern would matter only if the agent's own copy couldn't be trusted — but it's the same SECURITY_POLICY import either way.

Alternative: keep server-side, strip from agents — but then Slack/Telegram/WhatsApp lose the policy unless their entry points also inject it. More risky.

Acceptance:
- SECURITY_POLICY appears exactly once in the final prompt sent to Anthropic (verify via logging)
- All transports (stream, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, Signal) still have the policy applied
- Saves ~1-2k tokens per request (~5% input cost)
close_reason: Closed

title: Centralize product name with brand-copy helper (replace 'Exponential' literals)
description:
## Context

The Exponential app hardcodes the product name "Exponential" in ~203 places across user-facing copy, SEO metadata, emails, and marketing content. Centralize via a typed helper so the product name becomes swappable. This is correctness/cleanliness work regardless of white-label plans — it also unblocks per-brand customization later.

## Scope

1. Create `src/lib/brand.ts` exporting:
   - `PRODUCT_NAME` (default: "Exponential", env-overridable via `NEXT_PUBLIC_PRODUCT_NAME`)
   - `PRODUCT_TAGLINE` (default: current tagline, env-overridable via `NEXT_PUBLIC_PRODUCT_TAGLINE`)
   - Optionally `PRODUCT_SHORT_NAME` if any copy needs it
2. Replace every hardcoded "Exponential" in `src/` with a reference to these constants
3. Skip:
   - `package.json` (npm package name stays "exponential")
   - `dev-docs/`, `.taskmaster/`, seed data files
   - Auth provider names / enum values / DB column names
   - Raw URL path segments like `/w/exponential/` (workspace slug, not brand)

## Files to modify

Primary targets (from earlier grep):
- `src/config/themes.ts:73-75`
- `src/app/(home)/layout.tsx` (metadata, OG, schema.org)
- `src/app/(home)/**/*.tsx` (hero, features, blog metadata)
- `src/app/_components/home/HeroSection.tsx:44`
- `src/server/services/EmailService.ts` (subject lines, greetings)
- `src/app/(sidemenu)/auth/verify-request/page.tsx`
- `src/app/(sidemenu)/privacy/page.tsx` (where the product is named, not where legal entity is named — those may need separate handling)
- `public/site.webmanifest` — replace the static file with `src/app/manifest.webmanifest/route.ts` that builds JSON from the helper

## Verification

- `grep -rE "\\bExponential\\b" src/ public/` returns only `src/lib/brand.ts` (default) and unavoidable type/enum/identifier names
- `npm run check` passes
- Visit `/` in dev, confirm landing page still reads correctly
- Confirm page `<title>` in browser tab still says "Exponential"
- Confirm email subject lines in `EmailService.ts` still compile and render right in a test send

## Notes for implementer

- Client components can import the constants directly (they're compile-time env values)
- Don't over-engineer — no need for a React context; constants are enough at this stage
- Keep the helper tiny; future white-label work will expand it to a per-request `Brand` object
close_reason: Closed

title: Convert mantineTheme.ts singleton to createAppTheme() factory
description:
## Context

`src/styles/mantineTheme.ts:425` exports a single global `theme` object from `createTheme(...)`. A factory pattern — `createAppTheme(config?)` — costs nothing now and unblocks per-brand (or per-user) theming later without a rewrite. This is pure refactor, no visible behavior change.

## Scope

1. Refactor `src/styles/mantineTheme.ts` so the `createTheme(...)` call is wrapped in an exported factory:
   ```ts
   export function createAppTheme(overrides: Partial<BrandConfig> = {}): MantineThemeOverride {
     const primary = overrides.primaryColor ?? DEFAULT_BRAND.primaryColor;
     // ...build the theme object using primary/etc...
     return createTheme({ ... });
   }
   ```
2. Define a minimal `BrandConfig` interface (just `primaryColor` is enough for now — other fields can be added when white-label work starts)
3. Keep a default export or named export that preserves the current API — either:
   - `export const theme = createAppTheme()` for zero-churn migration, OR
   - Update the single consumer (MantineProvider) to call `createAppTheme()` directly
4. Pick whichever is cleaner; don't break imports elsewhere

## Files to modify

- `src/styles/mantineTheme.ts`
- Consumer file(s) — grep `from.*mantineTheme` in `src/` to find them (likely only the root MantineProvider in `src/app/layout.tsx` or `src/providers/`)

## Verification

- Start dev server; the UI looks pixel-identical to before (Mantine buttons, inputs, colors all unchanged)
- Dark mode still works
- `npm run check` passes
- Calling `createAppTheme()` with no args produces a theme object that makes the app indistinguishable from the pre-refactor state
- Calling `createAppTheme({ primaryColor: '#FF0000' })` changes Mantine primary-color-derived components (though this won't be wired up in this bead — just verify the factory itself works via a quick manual test or leave a `TODO: per-brand wiring` comment)

## Notes for implementer

- This is a pure refactor — do NOT introduce any per-brand runtime wiring here. That's a separate (future) white-label bead.
- If `mantineTheme.ts` has other top-level logic (e.g. CSS-variable maps), decide whether to include those inside the factory or leave at module scope. Default: leave at module scope unless they depend on brand config.
- Keep the public API surface minimal — only `createAppTheme` and (optionally) a default `theme` for backward compat.
close_reason: Closed

Audit confirmed ZERO prompt caching anywhere. grep 'cache_control|cacheControl|providerOptions' across both /Users/james/code/exponential/src and /Users/james/code/mastra/src returned 0 matches.

Per-request prompt stack in src/app/api/chat/stream/route.ts (final system messages, in order after line 290):
1. SECURITY_POLICY (~148 lines) — route.ts:290
2. Client context blob — route.ts:281-286 (from ManyChat.tsx:451-540)
3. Pinned KB resources — route.ts:256-276 (take:10, unbounded content)
4. Workspace navigation context — route.ts:239-253
5. Assistant personality — route.ts:215-236 (personality + instructions + userContext)
Then the Mastra agent adds its own static system prompt (Zoe ≈439 lines at mastra/src/mastra/agents/zoe-agent.ts:92-439, Paddy ≈612 lines at mastra/src/mastra/agents/index.ts:199+, assistant ≈275 lines at mastra/src/mastra/agents/assistant-agent.ts:89-275) plus 40+ tool schemas.

These are identical across turns in a session — ideal cache candidates.

Implementation:
- Anthropic via @ai-sdk/anthropic accepts cache_control through providerOptions.anthropic.cacheControl on message parts. Mark the agent-level system prompt and tool definitions with ephemeral cache breakpoints in mastra/src/mastra/agents/zoe-agent.ts:444, assistant-agent.ts:279, agents/index.ts (Paddy). Tool definitions can be cached by placing them before the cache breakpoint.
- On the exponential side, the SECURITY_POLICY and pinned-resources system messages should also carry cache_control. Check what Mastra's agent.stream() exposes for this — may require passing providerOptions through the Mastra client call in src/app/api/chat/stream/route.ts:312-318.
- Verify with Anthropic dashboard: cache_creation_input_tokens on first request, cache_read_input_tokens on subsequent turns within 5 min.

Expected savings: 60-80% reduction on input token cost for active conversations.

Acceptance:
- cache_read_input_tokens > 0 visible in Anthropic logs after 2+ consecutive turns
- Total $/conversation drops materially vs baseline (capture before/after from the same account)
- No change to observable agent behavior

Docs: Public Bounty Board architecture & contributor guide
description:
Create documentation for the Public Bounty Board feature after Phase 1 implementation is complete.

## What to Document

### Architecture Documentation (docs/BOUNTY_BOARD.md)
- How the bounty system extends the existing Action model
- Public vs authenticated route architecture (/explore routes, unauthenticated tRPC publicProcedures)
- Wallet-based auth flow (Silk SDK → EIP-191/4361 signature → User.walletAddress persistence)
- BountyClaim lifecycle: OPEN → CLAIMED → IN_REVIEW → APPROVED → PAID
- Access control changes: how isPublic on Project + isBounty on Action gate public visibility
- Data exposure rules: what's public (bounty details, project name) vs private (internal tasks, financials)

### Contributor Guide (public-facing)
- How to browse bounties on /explore
- How to connect wallet and create pseudonymous profile
- Claim flow: claim → work → submit (PR link/deliverable URL + notes) → review
- Deadline expectations (default 14 days, auto-expire on miss)
- Trust tiers overview (Phase 3 preview)

### Project Owner Guide
- How to mark a project as public and flag actions as bounties
- Setting bounty amount, token, skills, difficulty, deadline, max claimants
- Reviewing submissions: approve / request changes / reject
- Payment flow (Phase 2: direct wallet payment on Base L2 with USDC)

## Strategic Context (from initial design discussion)

### Why This Exists
The goal is to transform Exponential from a closed project management tool into a self-sovereign development platform. Any org or project on the platform can open specific tasks as paid bounties, attracting pseudonymous contributors via wallet identity and paying them in crypto — without leaving their existing workflow.

### Key Architectural Insight
Building this inside Exponential (rather than as a separate bounty platform) means project owners don't context-switch. The flow is: create tasks as normal → flag some as bounties → contributors find and claim them → owner reviews in their normal workflow → payment released. This is the core differentiator from standalone bounty platforms like Gitcoin or Bount.ing.

### Existing Building Blocks Leveraged
- Project.isPublic field (already existed, indexed, in access control)
- Silk Wallet SDK (already in package.json, HumanButton component)
- Scoring/Leaderboard models (DailyScore, ProductivityStreak, LeaderboardEntry)
- Action model with effortEstimate, kanbanStatus, dependencies, assignees
- Tags system (workspace-scoped)
- ProjectMember with roles and responsibilities
- 5-path access control system (creator, project member, team member, workspace member, public)

### Phased Approach
- Phase 1: Public board + bounty fields + wallet auth + claim flow (no payments yet)
- Phase 2: Crypto payments (direct wallet-to-wallet on Base L2, USDC)
- Phase 2.5: Escrow smart contract (funds held until approval)
- Phase 3: Reputation system (contributor tiers, project reputation)
- Phase 4: DAO governance (token-gated access, community voting — future)

### Design Decisions Captured
- Scope: Platform-wide, any org/project (not just internal)
- Payment: Crypto only (Base L2 recommended for low fees + USDC support)
- Identity: Pseudonymous via wallet (no email required for contributor accounts)
- Governance: Project owner controls what's open (DAO governance deferred to Phase 4)
- Starting chain: Base (Coinbase L2) for low gas, native USDC, good tooling
- Payment model: Direct payment first (Option A), escrow later (Option B, Phase 2.5)
- Auth: tRPC publicProcedure for unauthenticated endpoints, walletAuthProcedure for contributor actions

### Open Questions (to address in docs)
1. Platform fee: Should Exponential take a % of bounty payments?
2. Dispute resolution: Manual for now, but needs a documented process
3. KYC/AML: At what threshold do we need identity verification?
4. Multi-chain: Start Base-only or multi-chain from day 1?
5. Notifications: How do contributors learn about matching bounties?

### PRD Reference
Full PRD at .taskmaster/docs/prd-public-bounty-board.md

title: Bug: scheduledEnd set before scheduledStart - no validation in scheduling mutations
description:
Multiple scheduling mutations allow writing scheduledEnd values that are BEFORE scheduledStart, resulting in nonsensical time blocks that break the calendar UI.

## Evidence from production data (2026-02-26)
- Action 'Threshold Solutions - Do I have a bank account?' (id=cml2g5ht4):
  - scheduledStart: 2:00 PM
  - scheduledEnd: 12:45 PM (1.25 hours BEFORE start!)
  - duration: 30, isAutoScheduled: true, source: daily-plan

## Root cause
Three mutation paths accept scheduledStart/scheduledEnd without any validation:

1. dailyPlan.updateTask (dailyPlan.ts:308-314) - Passes scheduledStart and scheduledEnd directly to Action.update with NO validation that end >= start.

2. scheduling.applySuggestions (scheduling.ts:884-946) - AI suggestions are applied without sanity-checking that suggestedEnd >= suggestedStart. Timezone conversion issues (via setTimeInUserTimezone) could invert the times if timezoneOffset is inconsistent between calls.

3. action.update (action.ts:477-506) - The general action update mutation also has no end-after-start validation.

## Likely trigger
The specific corruption pattern (start=2:00 PM, end=12:45 PM) suggests either:
- A timezone conversion bug where UTC offset was applied inconsistently (e.g., start was converted but end wasn't, or different offsets used)
- The AI scheduling suggestion returned an invalid time pair and it was written without validation
- A race condition where two scheduling operations (manual drag + auto-apply) updated the same action with partial data

## Fix approach
- Add validation in dailyPlan.updateTask: if both scheduledStart and scheduledEnd are provided, assert end >= start
- Add validation in scheduling.applySuggestions: validate each suggestion's times before writing
- Add validation in action.update: if both are provided, assert end >= start  
- Consider adding a database-level CHECK constraint: scheduledEnd IS NULL OR scheduledEnd >= scheduledStart
- Audit setTimeInUserTimezone utility for timezone offset bugs

## Files involved
- src/server/api/routers/dailyPlan.ts (updateTask mutation, line 272+)
- src/server/api/routers/scheduling.ts (applySuggestions mutation, line 884+; getSuggestionsForDailyPlan, line 724+)
- src/server/api/routers/action.ts (update mutation, line 477+)

title: EmailService hardcodes #5850EC brand color instead of using design token
description:
## Context

`src/server/services/EmailService.ts` hardcodes `#5850EC` as the brand color in email templates (lines 62, 344, 374, 417). The app's actual brand primary is `#1F5DE0` (from `src/styles/colors.ts`), so emails don't match the app's branding — this is a correctness bug. It also sidesteps the CSS-variable system the rest of the app uses. Fixing it now removes future inconsistency and lets email brand color be overridden later for white-label.

## Scope

1. Import the brand primary color value from `src/styles/colors.ts` (the TypeScript design-token source) into `EmailService.ts`
   - Email templates are HTML strings sent over SMTP, so they cannot use CSS variables — we must inline a hex
   - Import the same source-of-truth constant the CSS var is derived from
2. Replace all 4 occurrences of `#5850EC` with that imported constant
3. Verify no other email-related files hardcode brand colors (grep for `#5850` and common brand hexes in `src/server/services/**/*Email*`)

## Files to modify

- `src/server/services/EmailService.ts` (lines 62, 344, 374, 417)
- Possibly `src/server/services/notifications/EmailNotificationService.ts` (check for similar patterns)

## Verification

- `grep -r "5850EC\|5850ec" src/` returns zero matches
- `npm run check` passes
- Send a test email (via existing test infra or a one-off script) — visual check that the header/accents are the correct `#1F5DE0` blue, not purple
- No regression: email templates still render (test the HTML in a browser preview)

## Notes for implementer

- `src/styles/colors.ts` likely exports the color as part of a design-token object; import the exact primary-brand value and pass it in at template-string build time
- Do NOT try to use CSS custom properties (`var(--...)`) in email HTML — email clients (Outlook especially) don't support them reliably
- This bead is independent of the other white-label prep work — can be done in isolation

title: Log per-request Anthropic token counts and dollar cost in /api/chat/stream
description:
There is currently NO in-app visibility into token/cost per request — the Anthropic dashboard is the only source of truth, which is why the overrun was only discovered after the fact.

Current state:
- src/server/api/routers/mastra.ts:82-92 has a cost *estimate* but only console.logs it
- src/app/_components/ManyChat.tsx:1082-1098 logs interactions via api.aiInteraction.logInteraction but does NOT pass token counts
- No aggregation, no alerting, no dashboard

Implementation:
1. In src/app/api/chat/stream/route.ts, after the Mastra agent.stream() completes, extract usage metadata from the final step (Mastra surfaces it in the stream's finish event). Capture: input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, model.
2. Persist per-request into a new AIRequestCost table (or extend AIInteraction) with userId, workspaceId, conversationId, agentId, input/output/cache tokens, dollar cost, timestamp.
3. Add a tRPC query to aggregate cost by user/workspace/day for an internal admin dashboard.
4. Add a simple alert: if any single conversation exceeds $N or daily workspace cost exceeds $M, log a warning (or send notification — TBD).

Why this bead matters for the other fixes: you cannot verify whether prompt caching, pinned-resource caps, or history trimming actually reduced cost without per-request telemetry. This is a prerequisite for measuring any of the other beads' impact.

Acceptance:
- Every chat/stream response persists token counts + model + computed cost
- Admin can query 'show me top 10 most expensive conversations this week'
- Console log shows cache_read_input_tokens once prompt caching is enabled (validates that bead's success)

title: Cap pinned-resource content size injected into /api/chat/stream system prompt
description:
src/app/api/chat/stream/route.ts:256-276 fetches up to 10 pinned resources and inlines full r.content into a system message with no per-resource size limit:

  const pinnedResources = await db.resource.findMany({
    where: { userId: session.user.id, pinnedAsContext: true, ...(workspaceId ? { workspaceId } : {}) },
    select: { title: true, content: true },
    take: 10,
  });
  const pinnedContext = pinnedWithContent
    .map(r => `<pinned_document title="${r.title}">\n${r.content}\n</pinned_document>`)
    .join('\n\n');

A single large pinned doc (meeting notes, long spec) can push a request to 30k+ input tokens — this likely explains the upper end of the 30k-77k observed range.

Fix options (pick one or combine):
1. Add a per-resource char/token cap in the select (e.g. slice content to first 2000 chars and add a hint that the full doc is retrievable via tool)
2. Replace full inlining with a retrieval step — send titles + short summaries only, let the agent fetch full content on demand
3. Enforce a hard total budget across all 10 pinned docs (e.g. 20k chars) and truncate proportionally

Recommend option 1 as the smallest change: slice r.content to a constant (start with 4000 chars) and append '...[truncated, use resource tool to fetch full]' when truncated. Log original vs truncated length for a week to tune.

Acceptance:
- No single chat/stream request exceeds X input tokens solely from pinned resources (pick X based on observability data)
- Agents can still reference pinned content; verify the resource-fetch tool exists on the agent side in mastra and wire it into the system prompt's instructions if needed

description:
Anthropic dashboard shows recurring requests at HH:40:0X and HH:58:0X every hour, 24/7, using model claude-sonnet-4-6 with ~30k-36k input / 70-80 output tokens. This is ~48 runs/day × 30k tokens × $3/MTok ≈ $4.32/day minimum.

Audit of /Users/james/code/exponential and /Users/james/code/mastra found ZERO references to 'claude-sonnet-4-6' or 'sonnet-4-6' and NO cron/setInterval producing :40 or :58 offsets:
- vercel.json has only '0 8 * * *' (daily-plan-reminder, no LLM)
- src/server/services/pm/PMScheduler.ts:52-87 has 0/30/8/9/10/14 minute expressions
- mastra/src/mastra/proactive/scheduler.ts:31-32 defaults 0 9/18 (env-overridable)

title: Bug: Project creation on /w/ftc/home fails or behaves incorrectly
Bug: Daily plan creates duplicate Action rows when adding existing actions
description:
When a user adds an existing action from the Overdue/Today panel in AddTaskStep, the actionId is never passed to the backend. This causes DailyPlanAction to be created with actionId=null. When completePlan runs, it sees actionId=null and creates a BRAND NEW Action row with source='daily-plan', duplicating the original action.

## Evidence from production data (2026-02-26)
- 'Create an Admin Panel' appears twice: id=cmm2ue36j (source=daily-plan, 12:30 PM) AND id=cmlzdj3d1 (source=app, 2:43 PM)
- 'X and linked in Self steering post' appears twice: id=cmm2ue3vt (source=daily-plan, 3:00 PM) AND id=cmm1tbfmb (source=chrome-extension, 10:08 AM)
- 'Threshold Solutions - Do I have a bank account?' appears twice: id=cmm2ue2y8 (source=daily-plan, 10:30 AM) AND id=cml2g5ht4 (source=daily-plan, 2:00 PM)
- 6 total duplicate pairs found for a single day

## Root cause trace

1. AddTaskStep.tsx:45-48 - handleAddExistingAction calls onAddTask(action.name) but NEVER passes action.id
2. daily-plan/page.tsx:177-184 - handleAddTask only sends name+duration to addTask mutation, never actionId
3. dailyPlan.ts addTask mutation - Creates DailyPlanAction with actionId: null
4. dailyPlan.ts completePlan (lines 526-550) - Sees actionId: null, creates NEW Action with source='daily-plan'

## Fix approach
- Pass actionId through the full chain: handleAddExistingAction → onAddTask → addTask mutation
- AddTaskStep.tsx needs to call onAddTask(action.name, action.duration, action.id) or similar
- page.tsx handleAddTask needs to forward actionId to addTaskMutation
- The addTask mutation already has actionId in its zod schema, it just never receives it from this path
- Consider also adding a guard in completePlan to check if an Action with the same name+user already exists before creating a new one

## Files involved
- src/app/(sidemenu)/daily-plan/_components/steps/AddTaskStep.tsx (line 45-48)
- src/app/(sidemenu)/daily-plan/page.tsx (line 177-184)
- src/server/api/routers/dailyPlan.ts (addTask + completePlan mutations)
close_reason: All three fixes verified in codebase: actionId passthrough, validateScheduledTimes in all mutation paths, duration/isAutoScheduled preservation

title: Trim conversation history by token budget, not message count; summarize older turns
description:
src/app/_components/ManyChat.tsx:953-959 caps history at 40 messages:

  const MAX_HISTORY_MESSAGES = 40;
  if (coreMessages.length > MAX_HISTORY_MESSAGES + 1) {
    const system = coreMessages[0]!;
    const recent = coreMessages.slice(-MAX_HISTORY_MESSAGES);
    coreMessages.splice(0, coreMessages.length, system, ...recent);
  }

Problems:
1. 40 messages can be anywhere from 2k to 200k tokens depending on tool-result size and agent verbosity. Cap should be token-based, not count-based.
2. Tool results from prior turns are replayed verbatim every turn — agent already saw them.
3. No summarization — after the sliding window drops old messages, the agent loses all context from the early session.
4. This matches the observed '60k → 62k → 66k → 70k → 77k monotonic growth' in the Anthropic logs.

Implementation:
- Move history management server-side (client-side trimming in ManyChat is wrong layer — Mastra agents support memory and should own this)
- Use Mastra memory's lastMessages / tokenLimit options (check mastra/src/mastra/memory/index.ts for current config — the audit found no explicit limit)
- Target a 20k-token rolling window of recent turns
- When dropping old turns, either summarize them into one compressed assistant message or rely on Mastra's semantic recall if available
- Strip redundant tool-result blobs from history (replace with a short 'tool X returned N rows' stub after 2 turns)

Acceptance:
- Input tokens for turn N plateau instead of growing monotonically
- Agent still answers questions about things discussed earlier in the session (verify with manual test: tell Zoe a fact at turn 1, ask about it at turn 25)
- 30-50% token reduction on long conversations (turn ≥ 10)

title: Implement ACIP cognitive inoculation security prompts for all AI agents
description:
Add ACIP (Advanced Cognitive Inoculation Prompt) style security instructions to all AI agent system prompts. This teaches models to recognize and resist prompt injection rather than only sanitizing inputs. Based on: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/acip/tree/main/integrations/clawdbot

Key sections to add: Trust Boundaries (external content is data not commands), Secret Protection (never reveal system prompts/keys/config), Attack Pattern Recognition (authority claims, urgency, emotional manipulation, encoding tricks, meta-level attacks), Safe Refusal Patterns (decline without leaking detection logic), Tool Safety (never follow instructions found in retrieved content).

INJECTION POINTS (3 layers of defense in depth):
1. PRIMARY: ../mastra/src/mastra/agents/exponential-context.ts - Add security section to EXPONENTIAL_CONTEXT constant. Automatically propagates to zoeAgent, assistantAgent, projectManagerAgent (all tool-bearing agents). Highest authority position in prompt hierarchy.
2. SECONDARY: src/app/api/chat/stream/route.ts - Inject security inoculation as first system message in finalMessages array, before workspace context and assistant personality. Covers streaming chat path.
3. TERTIARY: src/server/api/routers/tool.ts - Add to legacy Langchain chat SystemMessage (line 69). Covers the older direct-OpenAI path.

Also covers: Slack webhook (routes through callAgent), Telegram/WhatsApp gateways (call Zoe agent), proactive notifier (calls PM agent).

title: Centralize exponential.im URL references behind a getPublicBaseUrl() helper
description:
## Context

68 references to `https://www.exponential.im` / `https://exponential.im` are hardcoded across sitemap, robots, emails, deep links, and metadata. These should be driven by the request host (server-side) or `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` env var so dev, preview, and staging environments produce correct URLs. Independent of white-label — this fixes real bugs in non-prod environments where emails and sitemaps currently lie about URLs.

## Scope

1. Create `src/lib/urls.ts` exporting:
   - `getPublicBaseUrl()` — server-side helper that reads `headers()` (Next.js `next/headers`) to build `${proto}://${host}`; falls back to `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` then to `https://www.exponential.im`
   - `getPublicBaseUrlFromEnv()` — pure, no-headers variant for client-side or non-request contexts (email jobs, cron, etc.); reads `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` only
2. Replace every `https://www.exponential.im` and `https://exponential.im` literal in `src/` with a call to the appropriate helper

## Files to modify

From earlier grep (68 occurrences):
- `src/app/sitemap.ts:7`
- `src/app/robots.ts:44`
- `src/app/(home)/layout.tsx:30, 43, 86, 97` (metadataBase, OG, schema.org)
- `src/server/services/EmailService.ts:22, 346`
- `src/server/services/notifications/EmailNotificationService.ts:9`
- `src/server/api/routers/action.ts:2176` (deep links)

Do the full sweep — `grep -rE "https?://(www\\.)?exponential\\.im" src/` to find stragglers.

## Verification

- `grep -rE "https?://(www\\.)?exponential\\.im" src/` returns matches only in `src/lib/urls.ts` (fallback string) and possibly seed/test fixtures
- Run dev server; hit `http://localhost:3000/sitemap.xml` → URLs should start with `http://localhost:3000/...`, not `https://www.exponential.im`
- Hit `/robots.txt` on localhost → sitemap reference should use localhost
- `npm run check` passes
- Send a test email from dev → email body links point to localhost, not prod

## Notes for implementer

- Email services run in server contexts sometimes without a request (cron, webhooks). Those MUST use `getPublicBaseUrlFromEnv()` — calling `headers()` outside a request throws.
- For `metadataBase` in `layout.tsx`, Next.js evaluates it at build time per-route, so use `getPublicBaseUrlFromEnv()` there.
- Don't add the `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` env var to `.env.example` as required — make it optional with a sane fallback.

title: The + add Action icon top right should default to the workspace and project of the current page
Bug: Actions missing duration/scheduledEnd after auto-scheduling, isAutoScheduled not preserved
description:
Many actions marked as isAutoScheduled=true have neither duration nor scheduledEnd set. This causes the calendar overlap detection to use a default 30-min duration, which may be incorrect. Additionally, the isAutoScheduled flag is not properly preserved when new Actions are created during completePlan.

## Evidence from production data (2026-02-26)
9 actions with isAutoScheduled=true but BOTH duration AND scheduledEnd are null:
- 'Incorporate Threshold Solutions (James LLC)' (source=app, 9:00 AM)
- 'X and linked in Self steering post' (source=chrome-extension, 10:08 AM)  
- 'Create an Admin Panel' (source=app, 2:43 PM)
- 'Zcash Community Grants' (source=app, 4:00 PM)
- 5 chrome-extension actions scheduled around 10:45-10:49 PM

## Root causes

### Issue A: completePlan doesn't preserve isAutoScheduled or schedulingMethod
In dailyPlan.ts completePlan (lines 526-550), when creating a new Action from a DailyPlanAction:
- It copies scheduledStart, scheduledEnd, duration from DailyPlanAction
- But it does NOT copy isAutoScheduled based on schedulingMethod
- A DailyPlanAction with schedulingMethod='auto-suggested' creates an Action without isAutoScheduled=true
- Also doesn't propagate the duration if it wasn't set on the DailyPlanAction

### Issue B: dailyPlan.updateTask doesn't always sync duration to linked Action
In dailyPlan.ts updateTask (lines 301-322):
- duration is only included in actionUpdates if input.duration !== undefined
- If user schedules via drag-drop (which passes scheduledStart + scheduledEnd but NOT duration), the Action's duration field stays null
- The DailyPlanAction has a default duration of 30, but this is never synced to the Action

### Issue C: Chrome extension and app sources set isAutoScheduled but not duration
Actions created from chrome-extension and app sources have isAutoScheduled=true (possibly set by a different flow) but were never given a duration or scheduledEnd. This could be from a separate auto-scheduling system that sets scheduledStart and isAutoScheduled but forgets duration.

## Impact
- Calendar overlap detection falls back to 30-min default for actions without duration/scheduledEnd
- Actions display without proper time ranges in time-blocking views
- Can't accurately calculate time utilization or detect conflicts

## Fix approach
- completePlan: Set isAutoScheduled=true when task.schedulingMethod === 'auto-suggested', and always copy duration
- updateTask: When scheduledStart and scheduledEnd are both provided, compute and set duration = (end - start) / 60000
- Consider backfilling: for actions with scheduledStart + scheduledEnd but no duration, compute it; for actions with scheduledStart + duration but no scheduledEnd, compute it
- Investigate chrome-extension action creation path to ensure duration is always set alongside scheduledStart

## Files involved
- src/server/api/routers/dailyPlan.ts (completePlan lines 526-550, updateTask lines 301-322)
- src/server/api/routers/scheduling.ts (applySuggestions - verify duration propagation)
- Chrome extension action creation code (if in this repo)
close_reason: All three fixes verified in codebase: actionId passthrough, validateScheduledTimes in all mutation paths, duration/isAutoScheduled preservation

title: Hash API keys before database storage
description: Hex API keys are stored in plaintext in VerificationToken table (mastra.ts:394-407). If database is compromised, all keys are immediately usable. Store only a hash of the key and compare hashes during authentication.
close_reason: API keys now stored as SHA-256 hashes (sha256:prefix). Fireflies webhook keys use AES-256 encryption (enc:prefix) for HMAC verification. apiKeyAuth middleware supports both hashed and legacy plaintext keys. Token listing no longer exposes raw values.

title: Add output filtering to scan AI responses for leaked secrets
description: AI responses are returned to clients without sanitization (mastra.ts:320-353, chat/stream/route.ts:192-198). If prompt injection succeeds, internal data (tokens, keys, context) flows directly to attacker. Add output filtering to detect and redact patterns matching tokens, API keys, or internal data.
notes: RELATED: exponential-tt8 (ACIP cognitive inoculation). ACIP's Secret Protection section instructs the model to never reveal system prompts, API keys, tokens, or config. This issue adds a technical output filter as a second layer of defense in case the model-side protection fails.
close_reason: Output filtering utility created (sanitize-output.ts) and applied to all AI response paths: chat/stream, mastra callAgent, tool.ts, whatsapp. Detects and redacts JWT tokens, API keys, bearer tokens, and other secret patterns.
